serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve carton of packaged unsweetened soy milk is approximately 250 ml (250 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast beverage or snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk (No Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for unsweetened packaged soy milk (cr_id D02030008). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 34 for unsweetened soy milk is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (34 × 2.46 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 0.8 g, consistent with negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (21 mg/100 g), no added sugar, and calcium fortification (126 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened packaged soy milk is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (21 mg/100 g), absence of added sugar, and calcium fortification (126 mg/100 g) support blood pressure management, and a 250 ml serving contributes only 53 mg sodium — well within DASH dietary targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (34), low carbohydrate (2.46 g/100 g), and high protein (3.26 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 34, only 2.46 g carbohydrate per 100 g, and 3.26 g protein per 100 g, unsweetened packaged soy milk produces negligible glycaemic impact and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; consumers should verify the 'no added sugar' label to distinguish from 'less sugar' variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.21 g/100 g), soy isoflavones with cardioprotective properties, and favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened soy milk is cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat (0.21 g/100 g), zero trans fat, soy isoflavone content (associated with LDL reduction), and a polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio of approximately 4:1; it is suitable as a regular beverage for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones (anti-inflammatory polyphenols) and calcium fortification (126 mg/100 g) for bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsweetened packaged soy milk is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; soy isoflavones provide anti-inflammatory benefit, and calcium fortification (126 mg/100 g) supports bone protection critical for patients on corticosteroid therapy — check label for vitamin D co-fortification to maximise benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soy Milk (No Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink plant-based beverage made from soybeans and water, commercially processed and sold in sealed cartons or bottles without any added sugar. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across East and Southeast Asia as a breakfast drink, snack, or dairy-free milk alternative. The beverage has a mild, slightly beany flavour with a smooth, thin-to-medium body. Per 100 g it provides 38 kcal, 3.26 g protein, 1.59 g fat (of which only 0.21 g is saturated), 2.46 g carbohydrate, 0.79 g naturally occurring sugar, 0.75 g dietary fibre, 21 mg sodium, and 126 mg calcium (often from fortification). Its glycemic index is 34 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) present in soy milk have documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. The product is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Consumers should verify the 'no added sugar' label to distinguish this product from 'less sugar' or sweetened variants. A standard serving is one 250 ml carton.
common_user_queries: Is soy milk good for diabetes?
Is unsweetened soy milk low GI?
How much protein is in soy milk?
Is soy milk suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of no-sugar soy milk?
Is soy milk good for heart health?
Can I drink soy milk if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the difference between no-sugar and less-sugar soy milk?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030008) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 34 corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for unsweetened soy milk (FDC ID 1097542) to supplement HPB data.
notes: Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database for this specific unsweetened packaged soy milk formulation — soy milk is not a significant dietary iron source and the value is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for comparable products. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g confirmed by product category (plant-based beverage, no hydrogenated oils). Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g as this is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol by nature. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 (data not available): detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific packaged unsweetened soy milk formulation are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database entry, and USDA FoodData Central values for comparable products vary by fortification level; consumers should consult product label for fortification details. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed as soy milk, while low-carbohydrate, is not conventionally classified as a ketogenic food in clinical or dietary guidelines. Potassium value of 110 mg/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 1097542) as a cross-reference supplement to HPB data.
